Sentence: You stand with your weight evenly split on the raised walkway, the planks slick beneath your boots.
Your chestnut hair is in its usual utilitarian bun; a single curl has slipped free and trembles in the
salt wind.
The neighborhood smells of kelp and diesel, a scent that folds comfort and warning into one breath.
You reach for the faded orange field notebook at your hip.
Sand has ground into the spine; the pages inside are haloed with fingerprints and hurried graphite.
When you open it, the familiar clutter of scrawled
equations and a list of names looks less like chaos and more like a map of obligations.
Your fingers find the solder-scarred multi-tool on its cord out of habit, an anchor against the small tremor in
your hands.
There is a box inside your ribs where your brother's last hour still fits, too precise to be accidental.
You have learned to move around it the way people walk around a pothole — slow, eyes
measure a piling, mark a rot, calculate the angle of a brace.
Practical steps don't ask for anything in return.
They don't require you to open
your mouth and say, Please.
You thumb through tide notes and sketches, lips forming words you won't send.
The Green Spire's distant silhouette is a darker smudge against the low sky, its vertical gardens a quiet promise of light and hands.
Elliot's patched smartwatch buzzes against your palm; his tidal readouts blink up like a small lighthouse.
Low tide window holds until about five.
Surge models haven't crept up since this morning, but overnight looks uncertain.
Good.
That gives me time to—' Your voice thins.
'I need to map pilings and get Sami's courier loop sorted for delivery routes.
I'll run an extra array tonight.
And I'll bring the salvage sensors from the Spire.
We can track real-time strain on the pilings.
it nudges, it tests, but you close your mouth instead of letting it swell into a request.
Saying yes feels like letting water into a room you've been sealing off.
You don't have to carry the whole thing alone, Maya.
We've got hands.
Let us do the heavy lifts.
Maya Rios (a little too brusque) I know.
I just—I'll handle the speech for tonight.
Technical points.
No theatrics.
Okay.
We'll still be there.
And...
if you want to run it by me, I can listen?
You want to say yes.
You want the simple warmth of someone listening.
You don't say it; you say, instead, 'I'll be at the Boardwalk Market to take notes.' The smartwatch vibrates with a small, patient beep and then goes quiet.
There you are.
I ran the eastern loop twice — deliveries, med pack drop, extra life vests at the old bakery.
People are nervous, but they're showing up to pack.
Good.
How's the courier network holding up with the storm sirens last week?
Stretching, but improvising.
We can reroute to the uptilt boardwalk if the pilings look too soft.
Also—' Sami hesitates, fingers worrying at a strap — 'Neighbors want to know if the council hearing's going to change anything.
You study the notebook until the letters blur into a grid of obligations.
The neighbors' question is both concrete and like a hand hovering by yours, waiting for permission to clasp.
You have practice with maps
and retrofits; you have less practice with the hard chord that is asking for help.
If we show them a plan—simple, modular—something they can replicate, it changes the conversation from 'save or sell' to 'repair together.'
That's the language people follow.
But they'll only come out if you make it something they can do themselves.
Show them the first bolt, Maya.
Show them it holds.
And if it doesn't?
Sami's smile softens.
'Then we fix it together.'
There is warmth in Sami's certainty that you can almost use to stitch up the hollow patch where your brother's memory sits.
Almost.
You turn a page and rehearse a sentence aloud, the sound of your voice strange in the open air.
Maya Rios (aloud, testing rhythm) If we don't act now, we cede the shoreline to plans that put profit before people.
A gull shrieks and your words hang, damp and light.
You try again, more technical Our pilings are compromised at 0.3 meters below the decking at eight of ten test sites; reinforcing with modular bracing reduces stress by—
Sami chuckles softly.
'You always go to numbers to hide the heart.'
You close the notebook and press your thumb to a smudge of graphite.
Numbers are scaffolding; they do not say the things your chest wants to skip.
Grief has taught you to catalog so that your hands have something to do while your mouth stays shut.
• Read the neighbor list aloud
You clear your throat and read the names — Mrs.
Lento, the Rosario twins, Mr.
Garza — their addresses are marks on a map and a permission to expect them.
Saying the names feels like an invitation.
• Sketch reinforcement diagram again
You pull a ruler from your jacket and redraw the bracing, angling supports and annotating weights.
The diagram is clean, exact—it's the sort of thing contractors respect.
Narrative continues
tired, steady, necessary.
Neighbors begin to trickle in — figures bending against the wind, a child clapping a small paddle, someone lugging a patched tarp.
None of them cross the line between watching and stepping in; they stand just
that asking might also bind you to them in a way that feels irrevocable.
A group of children paddle between half-submerged stalls, shouting about a makeshift raft.
A kayak glides past so close its shadow passes over the walkway.
You watch the shadow and feel, with an odd concreteness, how much of your plan depends on people deciding to show up and stay.
Elliot Chen walks up the elevated walkway from the direction of the Spire, hands stained with soil and a smudge of paint across his cheek.
He hums a tune you don't recognize; the sound is small and ordinary and catches like a lighter in wet tinder.
You look like you could use a cup of something hot.
Maya Rios (dryly) I'm holding heat in the notebook.
Then let the paper warm you.
Seriously — talk to me.
You are used to offering directives.
You are not used to requesting aid.
• Practice the closing line with Elliot
You whisper the last line, and Elliot repeats it back, softer, ironing the edges until it feels deliverable.
Hearing it out loud makes it less of a raw thing.
• Refuse the rehearsal and stick to the schematic
You shake your head and close the notebook, hiding the jagged end of the speech back in graphite.
Elliot's eyes search you for a crack and finds none.
Narrative continues
You try the closing line once, louder this time We are not asking for charity; we're asking for the right to stay, to repair, to inherit this shoreline.
the request for help.
Your chest tightens in a way that is almost, but not quite, familiar.
The old memory — the fast-onset storm, the wrong turn, the sound of water in places it should not be — sits directly
behind your ribs, an uninvited ballast.
You have been practicing resilience; it has too often meant carrying the weight alone because asking felt like losing the last piece of control.
come to the hearing, bring tools,
sign the petition.
You could ask Sami to shout for volunteers.
You could ask Elliot to put the sensor array out tonight and invite people to watch it hum.
Or you could fold the notebook shut and walk back to the Spire and make the repairs yourself, the work a language you already know.
You let the thought rise and don't stop it.
Something in you recognizes that the shoreline you are too afraid to cross is not only a physical boundary; it's a line between survival alone and survival
together.
The decision is not in technical graphs or tide tables.
It's here, in breath and willingness.
You breathe in, taste of salt and diesel, and practice the opening of your speech once more.
Your voice is steadier now, but your mouth still wants to keep certain words safe.
You feel the slightest tremor of pressure — the tide of a community moment about to edge in.
You press your hand flat on the notebook cover and think—briefly, unbearably—that everything depends on other people deciding to show up.
The realization sits between your shoulder blades, an ache of both dread and something like hope.
You straighten.
The crowd breathes out with you, an invisible swell of attention.
The line between mapping and asking narrows into a single step you have not yet taken.
You open your mouth.
Prototype tonight.
The Spire smells like damp soil and sawdust, and the air
inside is warmer, humid and alive — the kind of warmth that makes your fingers remember how to untie knots.
Light passes through moss-filtered skylights in a soft green wash; shelves of jars glitter with cultured
water, and a tangle of rope and reclaimed rebar leans against a bench like sleeping animals.
You set your field notebook back into its strap because tonight the plans are physical.
You can feel the loosened thread of the crowd’s attention from the previous hour still knotting around your ribs.
Saying things
in public was a test; getting your hands dirty is another.
Both require you to leave the safety of maps and go where people can see you falter.
There you are.' (He pushes his drafting glasses up with two fingers; sand flecks like constellation dust in his fringe.) 'I wasn't sure you'd come.
You sent three words and a blinking emoji.
That's not—' Your voice narrows; you are sharp because this is easier than the other kind of ask.
'—an invitation.
(He grins, small and apologetic.) 'It's a prototype night.
A prototype is a promise that you can measure.
Easier to debate than feelings, right?' He taps the mockup, a strip of living matrix woven
with root bundles and salvaged polymer seams.
'Roots as tensile loads.
Living riprap.
The model's only half the story.'
You crouch across from him, fingers hovering near the mockup without touching.
The miniature roots look stubborn and earnest.
Elliot Chen's eyes brighten when he talks about them; his sentences come out in green metaphors that make you want to roll your eyes and then, against expectation, listen.
Plants distribute force in ways we don't always give them credit for.
You let them hold tension, and they hold memory — if we bind old pilings to living crowns, the wall becomes a neighbor, not an appliance.
Or it becomes more competent at failing in untested conditions.' You let the skepticism in your tone do the work that caution usually does.
'What are your failure modes?
How does it respond to a ten-year surge?
To debris impact?
(He nods, patient.) 'We've simulated for surge envelopes and debris strike.
The matrix flexes; the root mass dampens shear.
We still need field data.
Which is why—' He taps his patched smartwatch as if
it can make the data pop into being.
'—we need tonight's sensor run.
The array, the flow gauges.
Noor's logs will tell us what to watch.'

I ran two retention scenarios this afternoon.' (Her voice is even, clinical, a counterpoint to Elliot Chen's warmth.) 'If we prioritize modular installation at the storm thresholds, projected run-off lowers by seventeen percent in the modeled grid.
But that's conditional — soils, compaction, maintenance.
The community has to commit to long-term upkeep or the model decays.
(Quiet) 'Maintenance is the hard line.
Not building it — keeping it alive after the cameras leave.'
(She doesn't look up immediately; when she does, her gaze is steady.) 'Exactly.
Which is why coupling with local labor programs and modular design matters.
This isn't about hero projects; it's about durable practices.'
You run a thumb along the edge of the mockup, thinking about rot and the loyalty you owe to neighbors who trusted you with their roofs.
Elliot Chen watches your hand with something like wonder.
The mild tension in your chest loosens a fraction; plans are things you can name.
• Run a finger through the model's root mass
Your fingertip catches a thread of preserved root; the miniature flexes under pressure.
Elliot Chen laughs like a small bell—relief and delight—while you feel a quiet, almost ridiculous kinship with the thing.
• Ask Elliot for the simulation output now
Elliot Chen reaches for his holopad and taps through charts, talking you through the numbers.
His voice is eager and precise; even when you probe the weak points, he answers without defensiveness.
The scene continues with attention moving around the bench and a tablet catching your eye.
You glance toward a corner where someone left a tablet open.
Irene Voss's glossy renderings slide across the screen in polished layers—slick animation of broad concrete swathes, tidy promenades, and tidy headlines.
They look like a
promise you have taught yourself to resist and also like the kind of thing people who want to be seen crave.
Across the bench, small scale models with mahogany veneers glint in the lamplight; Luca's aesthetic
hand is obvious even if he isn't standing at the table.
Who's—
(Softly) 'Renderings are seductive.
We can't let the gloss set our agenda.' (He smiles too easily and then steadies.) 'But they also tell us what language to fight in.
If their pitch is 'progress as spectacle', ours is 'progress as neighborhood.''
Okay!
Locations for tomorrow—Pier 6, the flooded wing of the Market, two barricade points by the old pumping station—' (She slams a handwritten list on the bench.
The paper flutters and the little jars collect in its wake.) '—and we need volunteers to bring tools.
I've got six kayaks and twelve people willing to chain themselves to—
Sami.' The single name holds more than reprimand; it holds calculation.
'We need presence at the hearing, not just a spectacle.
The council moves on recorded testimony.
(She bristles, hands on hips.) 'Presence, spectacle — same work.
People show up more when they can see the river making them hungry.
They're not going to read graphs.
They'll watch a line of bodies.'
Both matter.' (Her voice is a scalpel.) 'Public demonstration creates political weight.
Empirical evidence creates policy foundation.
We need to marry them.
(She throws a look at you, all fire and history.) 'You led the last sandbag run.
You can lead this.
Rally them, tell them what's at stake, and we'll back you.'
tactile, fragile, possible.

The room follows the cadence of your lists; practical certainty is a quieter binding, but binding nonetheless.
Sami complains about the paperwork but begins to fill slots.
The group begins assigning roles and gathering materials as the plan takes shape.
who can weld, who has tide sensors, where to source pilings, which neighbors are willing to host workshops.

and practical.
The Spire feels smaller and fuller at once, like a room drawn to a single point.
Luca's models gleam again in the corner, anonymous and bright.
Whoever left them traded their tact for shine; it doesn't reduce their power.

deadlines, budget lines, maintenance viability.

We can get the prototype installed at Market Edge overnight.

But I won't toss a plan into a room without the facts.
We need the installation to be defensible and repeatable.' Your fingers press harder into your notebook; the spine creaks.
Council hearing is two weeks.' She says it like a deadline, not a suggestion.
'That's our window to collect baseline runs and refine the prototype.
If we can present a tested modular and a community maintenance plan, the argument becomes harder to ignore.
earnest, exhausted, sharp.

It's logistical and personal
at once — help me do this, help me trust that we can keep it alive after the halo of attention fades.
You think of the line you almost spoke in public, the pull between stepping forward and fixing things alone.
The room waits, not impatient but expectant, like tide-locals listening for the tide to decide its own time.

loud, but tenacious.

two weeks to turn a prototype into proof, and you the fulcrum.

not heroic, not solitary —
structural.

Ten minutes.
The number arrives in the same way tide data has always arrived to you — not as a distant prediction but as a wave with teeth.
Ten minutes to hold a coastline in public
language, to make people who prefer graphs feel the weight of a home that smells of kelp and frying oil.
broad machines, concrete

of the chin, like a craftsman handing over a model and asking, without words, Will you take it?

The moment tightens; you return attention to the chamber.
You breathe.
The building's acoustics make your inhale loud.
This is more than diagrams; it is a ledger of who you are willing to trust with the future.
You rise when your name is called.
Your voice wants to keep some words in a safe pocket, but the room expects you to unfold everything you know.
You open your mouth and the first sentence lands in the chamber with the clean, inevitable sound of a dropped tool.
Councilmembers, neighbors — the Harborfront is not a problem to be moved.
It's a system: homes, boats, small businesses, people who have responded to the sea for generations.
Our proposal starts where the water meets life — a living seawall prototype, community labor credits, and a retrofit pilot for the most vulnerable terraces.
A murmur; a soft shuffle like wind through reed.
You feel, behind the professional cadence, the memory you do not want to carry into the room—the laugh of your brother, the way he sprinted when water
rose too fast and you couldn't reach him in time.
The memory is a stone you must place precisely or it will break the surface tension of what you say next.
data points that anchor hope in numbers.
Elliot Chen had uploaded the prototype's sensor run; Dr.
Noor's scenarios bracket the worst cases and the likely outcomes.
You let them breathe into your language.
Our sensors—' (you reach to the tablet) '—show early root adherence within seventy-two hours in simulated surge conditions.
With community labor credits, we can deploy local teams, keep costs low, and bind maintenance to the people who will live with it.
The retrofit pilot reduces interior flood risk for sixty-eight percent of units in the at-risk terraces on initial modeling.
The tidal projections are convergent with our model.
Given phased monitoring, the prototype provides both mitigation and data for scaled deployment.
It's low-cost, adaptive, and offers less ecological disruption than concrete barriers.
Councilperson Ms.
Rios, how do you mitigate for rapid-onset events?
Make them see people, not pixels.
You modulate.
You fold in a line about volunteers and neighborhood maintenance, about training that doubles as employment.
You mention the labor credits again because it is the hinge between civic infrastructure and livelihood — between technical efficacy and dignity.
Elliot Chen, when you gesture toward the tablet, offers another quick exchange at your elbow.
If we add live telemetry during the pilot, we can prove adaptive response.
It writes the maintenance schedule for us and shows the council the savings.
It buys the city learning in months, not years.
It keeps displacement off the table by using salvaged materials and local labor.
The chamber shifts.
A section of the council leans toward the promise of innovation; another bristles at the implication that grassroots teams can be trusted with public safety.
Luca rises then, silky and precise.
With all due respect, prototypes are romantic until a storm hits.
Our design guarantees shoreline recovery—fast.
Funding that comes through private partnerships can be mobilized in months, not seasons.
It returns property to productive use.
a flat, professional certainty that leans on precedent as if precedent were a law of nature.
We cannot risk experimenting on people's homes when the next season could mean catastrophic losses.
The project I propose is financed and fast.
It secures jobs.
It secures the waterfront.
financial security on one side, community agency on the other.

• Call out the displacement risk directly
You let the word 'displacement' land like a bell.
A councilmember frowns; a developer in the room shifts.
Sami's jaw tightens and you see someone in the gallery start to whisper.
The word gives faces to your data.
• Emphasize the job-training angle
You pivot to the labor credits and the training program.
The word 'jobs' softens some stiff faces; a few council aides jot numbers.
Elliot's eyes shine a fraction — practical language opening practical doors.
The choice reframes the room's reaction; you continue your testimony.
she frames urgency and scale as moral imperatives.
Luca places an offer on the table—private funding, expedited construction, a rosy economic forecast.
The clauses about eminent sale are thinly stated, paper-thin, and you feel the breath of compromise across the room.
A councilmember asks you a direct question about governance How will you ensure accountability and maintenance?
Community stewardship, public monitoring, and transparent reporting.
We couple volunteer labor with paid oversight positions funded through reduced insurance costs and maintenance credits.
The system is designed to be resilient and to grow with data.
Another hand shoots up.
'And if a prototype fails?'
Your pulse answers before your mouth does.
The chamber's acoustics make each inhalation a public act.
You think of the worst-case simulations and Dr.
Noor's cautious optimism.
You think of the practical, honest sweat of Elliot
Chen’s team at the Spire — nights spent wiring sensors and seeding moss.
You think of Sami's hands, always ready to paddle into something dangerous for a neighbor.
Dr.
Noor stands briefly to reinforce the point, her tone softening with the weight of long experience.
Every engineered system can fail.
The point of a pilot is to learn and to adapt.
If you build only what past precedent dictates—concrete and absolute—you lose the chance to reduce harm without wholesale displacement.
Adaptive systems give you the chance to iterate toward safety.
Elliot Chen leans forward from the gallery, his voice carrying an earnestness that slices through formality.
We can get demonstrable, city-credible results within a season.
Give us oversight, give us a probe area, and we'll deliver monitoring reports that matter.
You feel the room compress.
The clock on the dais ticks in a sound that now matches the music under your ribs.
Hearts in the gallery beat like carp under skin.
The tension is dense and electric; every sentence becomes a lever.
conservation with community strength; public escalation; or pragmatic compromise.
Each requires a different trust, a different kind of letting go.
- Stand with Elliot and push for a community-led pilot — trust neighbors, prototype first, hope to prove and protect without displacement.
- Join Sami and Dr.
Noor; escalate public pressure and demand hearings — raise the stakes in public, risk polarizing moderates but possibly force more transparency.

- Negotiate with Irene Voss and Luca for funded infrastructure in exchange for concessions — secure immediate resources, accept political and ethical cost, and hope to protect homes through compromise.
The chamber is louder than before; rain against the windows has become a drumroll.
Your hands want to do what they always do — build something with your own fingers until it will hold.

it asks you to choose whom you will bind into the work.

quiet, the way people are before a lab door opens.

Hope sits at the center of the pressure like a buoy.
You feel its buoyancy — positive, fierce — and the rapidness of your heartbeat forces clarity out of fear.
the choice will map the next season of work, the next wave of neighbors saved or uprooted, the trust you are willing to open yourself to.

everyone's done with endless plans that never outlast the next storm.

a miniature horizon of concrete ridges, gleaming membranes, and neat little parks suspended like afterthoughts.
